model_summary: Select six complaint-derived criteria (difficulty initiating tasks, substituting with low-effort rewards, digital distractibility, loss of time awareness, deadline-triggered panic, and uncertainty/self-blame) and nine modifiable predictors focused on practical, high-signal behavioral levers: implementation intentions, structured focus blocks/timers, digital-distraction (passive phone) metrics, time-awareness checks, contingency/deadline planning (cognitive reappraisal), self-compassion practice, external checklists/offloading, current energy level, and last-night sleep quality. Sampling prioritizes momentary EMA (1–9 Likert) across 6 prompts/day with passive phone aggregation per prompt and a single morning diary for sleep to preserve within-person variance and enable gVAR at short lags (mainly 0–6h, sleep effects 0–24h). The predictor set emphasizes non-redundant, modifiable psychological & environmental strategies with passive triangulation of digital distraction to reduce measurement error.
variable_selection_notes: All six complaint-derived criteria (C01–C06) are retained to preserve full complaint resolution. Predictor selection prioritized high HyDe/LLM signals (implementation intentions, structured focus/micro-tasks, digital distraction reduction, time-estimation training, cognitive restructuring/self-compassion, external offloading/checklists, social/accountability, and sleep/energy) while avoiding many near-duplicate executive-function constructs. Trade-offs: several high-ranked ontology leaves pointed to many overlapping CBT/self-management subnodes; I collapsed close candidates (e.g., task breakdown, graded assignment, and activation design) into 1–2 pragmatic predictors (implementation intentions and structured focus blocks) to limit collinearity and maintain estimability. Passive phone signals were included to objectively capture distraction (reduces reliance on self-report). Uncertainty: social accountability can be beneficial or stress-inducing (nonlinear effects) so its sign is treated cautiously. Missingness and platform-API limitations are the main measurement risks.

momentary_ema.notes: Prompts ask about events/behaviors 'since last prompt' on 1–9 Likert scales to increase within-person variance. Use small jitter inside blocks to avoid predictability.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary collects last-night sleep duration and quality, which are used as a predictor for daytime initiation/attention outcomes (lag 0–24h).

passive_streams.notes: Phone screen_time_minutes and unlock_count aggregated over the past ~3 hours (aligned to each EMA prompt). Ensure privacy (do not capture content) and battery-aware sampling.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Per-person longitudinal data: 28 days × 6 prompts = 168 timepoints; with missingness it's realistic to expect ~100–150 usable timepoints. Use within-person z-scoring and detrending if multi-day trends appear. Keep variable count moderate: 6 criteria + 9 predictors = 15 nodes; consider shrinkage/GAM-VAR or regularized gVAR for stable estimates.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 anchored Likert for EMA items (increases continuous approximation). Include passive continuous signals (minutes, counts) and daily sleep duration for additional variance. Encourage fine-grained response anchors (e.g., 1 = 'Not at all', 5 = 'Somewhat', 9 = 'Extremely').
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Risk factors: prompt fatigue (6/day), passive-sensor permissions revoked, platform API variations. Mitigations: brief items (<15s), occasional compliance prompts, and incentivize morning diary completion. Monitor and impute with state-space or multiple imputation tuned for time-series.

safety_notes: No acute self-harm or severe psychiatric risk is evident in the complaint text. If panic episodes are frequent/intense or there is functional impairment beyond typical distress, advise contacting a mental health professional. Passive collection must follow privacy/consent rules (no content capture). Avoid recommending medication changes; if pharmacologic treatment is suspected necessary, refer to a clinician.

criteria_variables.label: Failure to initiate intended task

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, when you sat down to begin a planned task, how much did you actually start the intended task within a few minutes? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Immediately and fully)
measurement.notes: Captures initiation intensity/latency as an approximate continuous measure; if a task attempt did not occur, report 1.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person mean-centering and z-scoring; inspect for linear trend across days

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / neurodevelopmental disorders / attention-deficit or hyperactivity disorder (adhd) / unspecified adhd / *initiation_difficulty
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable C01 corresponds to initiation failure. The measurement item assesses initiation of a planned task since the last prompt on a 1-9 scale, which directly operationalizes 'initiation'. Among candidates, the leaf initiation_difficulty explicitly matches this construct, offering the most specific alignment to the variable's intended meaning and the EMA-based assessment context (since_last_prompt, 6 prompts/day). This is the most precise operational counterpart for 'Failure to initiate intended task'.

criteria_variables.label: Substitution with low-effort rewarding activities

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you engage in easier, immediately rewarding activities (e.g., reorganizing photos, watching videos) instead of the task you intended? (1 = Not at all, 9 = A lot)
measurement.notes: If both task and substitutes occurred, rate relative dominance of substitutes.

criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable characterizes substitution with easy, immediately rewarding activities (e.g., watching videos) in place of the intended task. This maps most directly to the construct 'delaying_gratification' (self-regulation regarding reward postponement). Among candidates, idx=682 explicitly labels '*delaying_gratification' and is the closest operational leaf to capturing impulse-control/reward-substitution behavior described by C02. Although it is age-specific (middle_childhood), it provides the most specific match to the described mechanism (impulse control / reward-based substitution) within the available ontology leaves. This aligns with the pseudoprofile emphasis on modifiable self-regulation strategies and EMA-based assessment of reward-driven substitutions.

criteria_variables.label: Susceptibility to distraction by digital/novel stimuli

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how often did your attention get captured by phones/apps/novel stimuli interfering with the intended task? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Constantly)
measurement.notes: Triangulate with passive phone metrics (screen time and pickups) aggregated per prompt.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person standardization; consider log transform for skew in passive corroborates

criteria_variables.label: Loss of time awareness during off-task activity

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you lose track of time while doing other activities (i.e., time passed faster than expected)? (1 = Not at all, 9 = A great deal)
measurement.notes: Combine with passive screen_time to estimate objective durations.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; inspect for episodic spikes and consider threshold/event flags

criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable C04 measures loss of time awareness during off-task activity, captured by EMA self-report (how much time was lost track of while doing other activities). The most specific ontology leaf is time_perception_alteration (idx=6), which directly encodes alterations/distortions in time perception. This aligns with the measurement item (time perception distortion: “lose track of time” during off-task activity) and the mechanism (attentional drift leading to altered time awareness). Other leaves largely map to time management or attention control rather than perceptual timing, making time_perception_alteration the best single match.

criteria_variables.label: Acute panic/anxiety when deadlines are noticed

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how anxious or panicked did you feel specifically because of deadlines or time pressure? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Extremely)
measurement.notes: If a deadline-triggered panic attack occurred, rate intensity; also optionally flag event in free comment.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-scoring; consider event-flag modeling for extreme spikes

criteria_variables.label: Uncertainty and self-blame about cause of difficulties

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel unsure if this was laziness versus a fixable problem, and blame yourself? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Strongly uncertain/blaming)
measurement.notes: Captures rumination/self-judgment which can block selecting strategies.

predictor_variables.label: Implementation intentions / task-initiation cues
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Specific if-then plans (e.g., 'If I sit down at X, I will do Y for 5 minutes') reduce intention–action gap and cue initiation rapidly.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Create a brief if-then plan before work (e.g., 'When I open my laptop, I will spend 5 minutes on step 1').

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you form a specific if-then plan to start the next task? (1 = Not at all specific, 9 = Very specific/actionable)
measurement.notes: Directly captures planning strength rather than presence/absence.

expected_effects.rationale: Forms immediate cue that reduces failure to initiate within hours.
Reduces probability of substituting with easy rewards by increasing commitment to stepwise starts.
A clear plan focuses attention and reduces distractibility in the near term.
May reduce off-task drifting and subsequent time loss across the work period.
Successful initiation from plans can reduce deadline-triggered panic by decreasing accrued delay.
Actionable plans can reduce self-blame by producing observable change.

feasibility.notes: Short EMA item; likely good compliance.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is 'implementation_intention_ifthen_plans' (idx 4398) because the variable label 'Implementation intentions / task-initiation cues' directly corresponds to implementation_intention_ifthen_plans. The measurement item asks about forming a specific if-then plan since the last prompt to start the next task, which aligns with the mechanism (if-then planning reduces the intention–action gap by providing initiation cues). The EMA self-report format and 1–9 Likert on plan specificity fit this node precisely. Pseudoprofile context supports emphasizing modifiable planning strategies, aligning with this predictor without overfitting to other overlapping constructs.

predictor_variables.label: Structured focus blocks and micro-task timers
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Using timers and graded micro-tasks supports sustained attention and reduces switching to substitutes.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Commit to a 25-minute timer or a 10-minute micro-task with a visible countdown.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you use a timer or micro-task structure to focus? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Consistently used and adhered to)
measurement.notes: Captures use and adherence rather than theoretical awareness.

expected_effects.rationale: Timers encourage action immediately and sustain initial engagement.
Reduces substitution by structuring work into small achievable chunks.
Buffering attention with focused blocks reduces distractibility.
May reduce prolonged off-task episodes and time loss.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA question; actual timer usage may be validated by third-party app logs if available.

Measurement (EMA item: 'Since the last prompt, did you use a timer or micro-task structure to focus?') aligns with the predictor. Mechanism rationale (timers and graded micro-tasks support sustained attention) matches the leaf's focus. Pseudoprofile context supports time-blocking as a modifiable behavioral lever without overfitting.

predictor_variables.label: Digital distraction (passive phone metrics: screen time and pickups)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: High screen-time and frequent pickups indicate environmental/digital drivers of substitution and time loss; reducing pickups/screen time reduces distraction.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Enable Do Not Disturb, scheduled app limits, remove tempting apps from home screen.

measurement.channel: smartphone OS usage stats (screen_time_minutes, unlock_count) aggregated to EMA windows
measurement.item_or_signal: screen_time_minutes in past ~3 hours; unlock_count in past ~3 hours (collected via OS usage API; content not captured)
measurement.notes: Use privacy-preserving aggregation (minutes and counts). Normalize per-person for gVAR.

expected_effects.rationale: Higher recent screen time strongly correlates with substitution behavior.
Pickups and screen minutes directly index distractibility.
More distraction increases failure to initiate/continue intended tasks.
Longer screen sessions predict loss of time awareness.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure explicit consent for passive collection; avoid content-level monitoring.

feasibility.notes: OS-level access required; participant permissions and battery constraints may reduce sampling.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Selected leaf matches Digital distraction reduction via environmental_stimulus_minimization_strategy: it targets reducing digital stimuli (screen_time_minutes, unlock_count) through environmental changes; measurement is passive_phone_sensor; aligns with the variable label and mechanism (reducing pickups/screen time reduces distraction). This is the most specific operational node among candidates.

predictor_variables.label: Time-awareness checks / time-estimation practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Frequent clock-checks, countdowns or brief time-estimation practices recalibrate subjective time and reduce unnoticed long off-task periods.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Set visible countdown timers, pause to estimate elapsed time every 10–20 minutes.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how often did you check the time or use a countdown to monitor elapsed time? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Constantly)
measurement.notes: Can be augmented with clock-app open counts if available.

expected_effects.rationale: More frequent time checks directly reduce loss of time awareness in the near term.
Better time-awareness supports timely initiation and return to task.
Reduces long immersion in substitutes by prompting re-evaluation of elapsed time.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA item; clock-app opens may be noisy across device types.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Direct mapping: Time-awareness checks / time-estimation practice corresponds to the leaf time_estimation_strategy_training under PSYCHO / cognitive_and_metacognitive_interventions / cognitive_remediation_and_rehabilitation. The variable label aligns with time-estimation strategy training, and the EMA self-report measure item (frequency of time checks or countdown use since the last prompt) matches the leaf’s domain. Mechanism aligns: frequent time checks recalibrate subjective time and reduce unnoticed off-task periods. Pseudoprofile context supports selecting time-awareness as a modifiable cognitive strategy and prioritizes non-redundant predictors; this leaf is the most specific operational construct for the described variable.

predictor_variables.label: Contingency planning / deadline reframing (cognitive restructuring)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Pre-emptive planning and cognitive reframing reduce acute deadline-triggered panic by converting threat into manageable steps.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Brief reappraisal of deadline meaning (e.g., 'I can prioritize the next immediate step') and if-then contingency plans.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you do any contingency planning or reframe upcoming deadlines (e.g., plan next steps, reappraise) ? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Very much)
measurement.notes: Targets the cognitive step expected to reduce panic when deadlines appear.

expected_effects.rationale: Reappraisal and contingency plans directly reduce acute deadline-related anxiety in the short term.
Contingency planning may increase perceived ability to act, thereby improving initiation (sign may be indirect; increased planning can precede action).
Reduces uncertainty/self-blame by creating concrete next steps.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Cognitive restructuring items could transiently raise distress if recalling stress; ensure supportive instructions.

feasibility.notes: Short EMA question acceptable; therapist-guided training may increase effect sizes later.

Measurement (EMA self-report: since last prompt, extent of contingency planning or deadline reframing) aligns with cognitive reframing of intentionality as the mechanism to convert deadlines into manageable steps. Therefore this leaf provides the most specific operational construct for the variable.

predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Self-compassion reduces self-blame and rumination, supporting adaptive selection of corrective steps and reducing avoidance.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short self-compassion prompts (e.g., 'What would I say to a friend in this situation?').

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you practice self-compassion or intentionally reduce self-blame? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Very much)
measurement.notes: Captures active practice (not trait self-compassion).

expected_effects.rationale: Directly targets self-blame and uncertainty, reducing their intensity.
Reduces emotional reactivity to deadlines via affect regulation.
By lowering avoidance, may indirectly improve initiation (effect may be indirect).

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: May briefly surface negative self-evaluations; framing should be supportive.

predictor_variables.label: External cognitive offloading / checklists
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Using checklists or stepwise external supports reduces reliance on internal working memory and lowers activation energy for task starts.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Use a 3-step checklist posted near work space (e.g., open doc, write title, write first sentence).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you use an external checklist or offloading tool to start/structure the task? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Used and followed closely)
measurement.notes: Designed to capture external scaffolding use.

expected_effects.rationale: Offloading reduces friction to initiating tasks.
Structured steps channel behavior away from substitutes.
Tangible progress reduces self-blame via feedback.

predictor_variables.label: Current energy / alertness level
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Momentary energy/alertness moderates ability to initiate and resist low-effort substitutes; influenced by sleep, nutrition and circadian factors.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short behavioral changes: hydration, brief movement break, or short nap (outside work contexts) to restore energy.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now (since the last prompt), how would you rate your energy/alertness? (1 = Exhausted, 9 = Very high energy)
measurement.notes: Energy is a proximal moderator for initiation and distractibility.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; consider controlling for circadian time-of-day

expected_effects.rationale: Higher energy is expected to reduce failure to initiate in the short term.
Low energy increases reliance on low-effort substitutes.
Better energy improves sustained attention against digital pulls.

feasibility.notes: Short momentary item with high compliance expected.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable label is Current energy / alertness level. Measurement item matches exactly: 'Right now (since the last prompt), how would you rate your energy/alertness? (1 = Exhausted, 9 = Very high energy)'. The most specific operational predicate in the candidates is ema_mental_energy (ecological_momentary_assessment_ema / momentary_cognition_and_energy / ema_mental_energy), which aligns with momentary energy assessment via EMA in the BIO domain. This leaf is under ecological_momentary_assessment_ema, reflecting a proximal energy measure compatible with the pseudoprofile's emphasis on momentary energy as a moderator of initiation and distractibility. Thus it is the best-matching, most specific predictor node for this variable.
predictor_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Current energy / alertness level | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Momentary energy/alertness moderates ability to initiate and resist low-effort substitutes; influenced by sleep, nutrition and circadian factors. | Intervention examples: Short behavioral changes: hydration, brief movement break, or short nap (outside work contexts) to restore energy. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Right now (since the last prompt), how would you rate your energy/alertness? (1 = Exhausted, 9 = Very high energy) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Glymphatic_Support / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices (proximal energy measure)

predictor_variables.label: Last-night sleep quality and duration (morning diary)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Sleep influences daytime cognitive control and energy; poor sleep can increase distractibility and reduce initiation.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Consistent sleep schedule, sleep hygiene changes (no electronics before bed), environmental adjustments.

measurement.channel: morning diary in smartphone EMA app
measurement.item_or_signal: Last night: how many hours did you sleep? ______ minutes. How would you rate your sleep quality? (1 = Very poor, 9 = Very good)
measurement.notes: Use duration + quality; primary lag expected 0-24h to daytime outcomes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: duration: none or standardized; quality: z-score; control for weekday effects

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces failure to initiate across the next day.
Improves self-control against low-effort substitutions the following day.
Improves sustained attention.
Better sleep reduces time-loss episodes.

feasibility.notes: Single morning diary item; high expected completion if kept brief.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Selected leaf 4549: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / self_assessment_and_logs / daily_and_retrospective_logs / sleep_diary. This directly encodes 'last-night sleep quality and duration' as a morning diary self-report. It provides both duration and quality via a diary-style self-assessment, aligning with the var's measurement item (hours slept, sleep quality 1-9) and the morning diary channel used in EMA. The variable label and mechanism (sleep influences daytime cognitive control and energy) are matched by this sleep_diary construct, which also fits the pseudoprofile's emphasis on high-signal, modifiable self-monitoring with EMA. Other candidates (e.g., sleep_timing_log, circadian_sleep_tracking) are less precisely aligned with the 'morning diary' aspect and/or lack the explicit diary/log framing for last-night sleep.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 (implementation intentions), P02 (structured focus), and P07 (checklists) are conceptually related and may show moderate collinearity.
P06 (self-compassion) and C06 (self-blame) will be correlated; careful temporal ordering needed.
P03 passive phone metrics might correlate with EMA reports of distraction (C03), requiring careful modeling to avoid measurement redundancy.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: Passive screen_time minutes could be zero during some windows (floor).
Highly motivated participants may show ceiling responses on implementation-intention or checklist items, reducing variance.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1–9 Likert anchors with behavioral anchors to increase spread.
Aggregate passive minutes over 3-hour windows to reduce zero-inflation but preserve variability.
Encourage participants to report brief, concrete episodes rather than binary yes/no to avoid floor effects.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Biomedical/neurostimulation interventions (e.g., TMS, pharmacotherapy) deprioritized at this initial behavioral gVAR stage due to scope and safety.
Highly overlapping executive-function subnodes (e.g., many fine-grained planning variants) collapsed into implementation intentions/structured focus to reduce redundancy.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 28 days and 6 prompts/day (168 max observations), expect ~100–150 usable timepoints after typical missingness. For a single-person gVAR with 15 nodes, regularization (LASSO/graphical-LASSO) or Bayesian shrinkage is recommended. Consider reducing nodes if per-person sample is much lower.

criteria.label: Failure to initiate intended task
criteria.query_text_used: Failure to initiate intended task | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, when you sat down to begin a planned task, how much did you actually start the intended task within a few minutes? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Immediately and fully) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures initiation intensity/latency as an approximate continuous measure; if a task attempt did not occur, report 1.

mapping.rationale: Variable C01 corresponds to initiation failure. The measurement item assesses initiation of a planned task since the last prompt on a 1-9 scale, which directly operationalizes 'initiation'. Among candidates, the leaf initiation_difficulty explicitly matches this construct, offering the most specific alignment to the variable's intended meaning and the EMA-based assessment context (since_last_prompt, 6 prompts/day). This is the most precise operational counterpart for 'Failure to initiate intended task'.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, when you sat down to begin a planned task, how much did you actually start the intended task within a few minutes? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Immediately and fully)
measurement.notes: Captures initiation intensity/latency as an approximate continuous measure; if a task attempt did not occur, report 1.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person mean-centering and z-scoring; inspect for linear trend across days

criteria.label: Substitution with low-effort rewarding activities
criteria.query_text_used: Substitution with low-effort rewarding activities | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you engage in easier, immediately rewarding activities (e.g., reorganizing photos, watching videos) instead of the task you intended? (1 = Not at all, 9 = A lot) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: If both task and substitutes occurred, rate relative dominance of substitutes.

mapping.rationale: The variable characterizes substitution with easy, immediately rewarding activities (e.g., watching videos) in place of the intended task. This maps most directly to the construct 'delaying_gratification' (self-regulation regarding reward postponement). Among candidates, idx=682 explicitly labels '*delaying_gratification' and is the closest operational leaf to capturing impulse-control/reward-substitution behavior described by C02. Although it is age-specific (middle_childhood), it provides the most specific match to the described mechanism (impulse control / reward-based substitution) within the available ontology leaves. This aligns with the pseudoprofile emphasis on modifiable self-regulation strategies and EMA-based assessment of reward-driven substitutions.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you engage in easier, immediately rewarding activities (e.g., reorganizing photos, watching videos) instead of the task you intended? (1 = Not at all, 9 = A lot)
measurement.notes: If both task and substitutes occurred, rate relative dominance of substitutes.

criteria.label: Susceptibility to distraction by digital/novel stimuli
criteria.query_text_used: Susceptibility to distraction by digital/novel stimuli | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how often did your attention get captured by phones/apps/novel stimuli interfering with the intended task? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Constantly) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Triangulate with passive phone metrics (screen time and pickups) aggregated per prompt.

mapping.rationale: Variable C03 'Susceptibility to distraction by digital/novel stimuli' maps to leaf 'distractibility_by_external_stimuli' (idx 28064), which captures distraction by external stimuli, including digital devices; aligns with EMA self-report item 'Since the last prompt, how often did your attention get captured by phones/apps/novel stimuli interfering with the intended task?' and the pseudoprofile context emphasizing digital distraction metrics. This is the most specific operational leaf matching digital distraction susceptibility among candidates; other leaves either address non-digital distraction, are ADHD-specific, or focus on mitigation rather than susceptibility.

source_variable.label: Susceptibility to distraction by digital/novel stimuli

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how often did your attention get captured by phones/apps/novel stimuli interfering with the intended task? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Constantly)
measurement.notes: Triangulate with passive phone metrics (screen time and pickups) aggregated per prompt.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person standardization; consider log transform for skew in passive corroborates

criteria.label: Loss of time awareness during off-task activity
criteria.query_text_used: Loss of time awareness during off-task activity | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you lose track of time while doing other activities (i.e., time passed faster than expected)? (1 = Not at all, 9 = A great deal) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Combine with passive screen_time to estimate objective durations.

mapping.rationale: The variable C04 measures loss of time awareness during off-task activity, captured by EMA self-report (how much time was lost track of while doing other activities). The most specific ontology leaf is time_perception_alteration (idx=6), which directly encodes alterations/distortions in time perception. This aligns with the measurement item (time perception distortion: “lose track of time” during off-task activity) and the mechanism (attentional drift leading to altered time awareness). Other leaves largely map to time management or attention control rather than perceptual timing, making time_perception_alteration the best single match.

source_variable.label: Loss of time awareness during off-task activity

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you lose track of time while doing other activities (i.e., time passed faster than expected)? (1 = Not at all, 9 = A great deal)
measurement.notes: Combine with passive screen_time to estimate objective durations.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; inspect for episodic spikes and consider threshold/event flags

criteria.label: Acute panic/anxiety when deadlines are noticed
criteria.query_text_used: Acute panic/anxiety when deadlines are noticed | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how anxious or panicked did you feel specifically because of deadlines or time pressure? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: If a deadline-triggered panic attack occurred, rate intensity; also optionally flag event in free comment.

mapping.rationale: Variable C05 corresponds to acute panic/anxiety triggered by deadlines/time pressure. The best-match leaf is cue_triggered_panic_attack_occurrence (idx 32132) within panic attacks: it represents panic episodes precipitated by a specific cue (e.g., deadlines/time pressure). The measurement item is EMA self-report with rating of anxiety/panic intensity since the last prompt (1-9), aligning with the cue-triggered mechanism. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for deadline-triggered panic and avoids broader, non-specific panic or somatic symptoms. The pseudoprofile context supports the selection as a complaint-derived criterion mapped to a cue-triggered panic mechanism; no better, more precise subnode exists in the list.

source_variable.label: Acute panic/anxiety when deadlines are noticed

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how anxious or panicked did you feel specifically because of deadlines or time pressure? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Extremely)
measurement.notes: If a deadline-triggered panic attack occurred, rate intensity; also optionally flag event in free comment.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-scoring; consider event-flag modeling for extreme spikes

criteria.label: Uncertainty and self-blame about cause of difficulties
criteria.query_text_used: Uncertainty and self-blame about cause of difficulties | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel unsure if this was laziness versus a fixable problem, and blame yourself? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Strongly uncertain/blaming) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures rumination/self-judgment which can block selecting strategies.

mapping.rationale: C06 targets uncertainty about the cause of difficulties and self-blame (rumination/self-judgment). The EMA item asks if difficulties are laziness vs a fixable problem and whether the person blames themselves, which aligns most closely with self-critical rumination. Among candidates, the leaf 'self_critical_rumination' (idx 19031) is the most specific operational construct matching this pattern, better than more general self-blame or forgiveness nodes.

source_variable.label: Uncertainty and self-blame about cause of difficulties

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel unsure if this was laziness versus a fixable problem, and blame yourself? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Strongly uncertain/blaming)
measurement.notes: Captures rumination/self-judgment which can block selecting strategies.

predictors.label: Implementation intentions / task-initiation cues
predictors.query_text_used: Implementation intentions / task-initiation cues | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Specific if-then plans (e.g., 'If I sit down at X, I will do Y for 5 minutes') reduce intention–action gap and cue initiation rapidly. | Intervention examples: Create a brief if-then plan before work (e.g., 'When I open my laptop, I will spend 5 minutes on step 1'). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you form a specific if-then plan to start the next task? (1 = Not at all specific, 9 = Very specific/actionable) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies / Behavior_Change_and_Activation / planning_and_grading / activation_design / *task_initiation_cues

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is 'implementation_intention_ifthen_plans' (idx 4398) because the variable label 'Implementation intentions / task-initiation cues' directly corresponds to implementation_intention_ifthen_plans. The measurement item asks about forming a specific if-then plan since the last prompt to start the next task, which aligns with the mechanism (if-then planning reduces the intention–action gap by providing initiation cues). The EMA self-report format and 1–9 Likert on plan specificity fit this node precisely. Pseudoprofile context supports emphasizing modifiable planning strategies, aligning with this predictor without overfitting to other overlapping constructs.

source_variable.label: Implementation intentions / task-initiation cues
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Specific if-then plans (e.g., 'If I sit down at X, I will do Y for 5 minutes') reduce intention–action gap and cue initiation rapidly.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Create a brief if-then plan before work (e.g., 'When I open my laptop, I will spend 5 minutes on step 1').

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you form a specific if-then plan to start the next task? (1 = Not at all specific, 9 = Very specific/actionable)
measurement.notes: Directly captures planning strength rather than presence/absence.

expected_effects.rationale: Forms immediate cue that reduces failure to initiate within hours.
Reduces probability of substituting with easy rewards by increasing commitment to stepwise starts.
A clear plan focuses attention and reduces distractibility in the near term.
May reduce off-task drifting and subsequent time loss across the work period.
Successful initiation from plans can reduce deadline-triggered panic by decreasing accrued delay.
Actionable plans can reduce self-blame by producing observable change.

feasibility.notes: Short EMA item; likely good compliance.

predictors.label: Structured focus blocks and micro-task timers
predictors.query_text_used: Structured focus blocks and micro-task timers | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Using timers and graded micro-tasks supports sustained attention and reduces switching to substitutes. | Intervention examples: Commit to a 25-minute timer or a 10-minute micro-task with a visible countdown. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you use a timer or micro-task structure to focus? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Consistently used and adhered to) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / behavioral_integration / time_blocking_for_valued_actions

mapping.rationale: Exact match: Structured focus blocks and micro-task timers map to time_blocking_for_valued_actions leaf under PSYCHO/change_process_and_therapeutic_phases/behavioral_integration/implementation_and_routine_building. Measurement (EMA item: 'Since the last prompt, did you use a timer or micro-task structure to focus?') aligns with the predictor. Mechanism rationale (timers and graded micro-tasks support sustained attention) matches the leaf's focus. Pseudoprofile context supports time-blocking as a modifiable behavioral lever without overfitting.

source_variable.label: Structured focus blocks and micro-task timers
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Using timers and graded micro-tasks supports sustained attention and reduces switching to substitutes.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Commit to a 25-minute timer or a 10-minute micro-task with a visible countdown.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you use a timer or micro-task structure to focus? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Consistently used and adhered to)
measurement.notes: Captures use and adherence rather than theoretical awareness.

expected_effects.rationale: Timers encourage action immediately and sustain initial engagement.
Reduces substitution by structuring work into small achievable chunks.
Buffering attention with focused blocks reduces distractibility.
May reduce prolonged off-task episodes and time loss.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA question; actual timer usage may be validated by third-party app logs if available.

predictors.label: Digital distraction (passive phone metrics: screen time and pickups)
predictors.query_text_used: Digital distraction (passive phone metrics: screen time and pickups) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: High screen-time and frequent pickups indicate environmental/digital drivers of substitution and time loss; reducing pickups/screen time reduces distraction. | Intervention examples: Enable Do Not Disturb, scheduled app limits, remove tempting apps from home screen. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | screen_time_minutes in past ~3 hours; unlock_count in past ~3 hours (collected via OS usage API; content not captured) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_capacity_enhancement_and_stimulation / cognitive_offloading_and_external_aids / attention_support_systems / reduce_distraction / *environmental_stimulus_minimization_strategy

mapping.rationale: Selected leaf matches Digital distraction reduction via environmental_stimulus_minimization_strategy: it targets reducing digital stimuli (screen_time_minutes, unlock_count) through environmental changes; measurement is passive_phone_sensor; aligns with the variable label and mechanism (reducing pickups/screen time reduces distraction). This is the most specific operational node among candidates.

source_variable.label: Digital distraction (passive phone metrics: screen time and pickups)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: High screen-time and frequent pickups indicate environmental/digital drivers of substitution and time loss; reducing pickups/screen time reduces distraction.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Enable Do Not Disturb, scheduled app limits, remove tempting apps from home screen.

measurement.channel: smartphone OS usage stats (screen_time_minutes, unlock_count) aggregated to EMA windows
measurement.item_or_signal: screen_time_minutes in past ~3 hours; unlock_count in past ~3 hours (collected via OS usage API; content not captured)
measurement.notes: Use privacy-preserving aggregation (minutes and counts). Normalize per-person for gVAR.

expected_effects.rationale: Higher recent screen time strongly correlates with substitution behavior.
Pickups and screen minutes directly index distractibility.
More distraction increases failure to initiate/continue intended tasks.
Longer screen sessions predict loss of time awareness.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure explicit consent for passive collection; avoid content-level monitoring.

feasibility.notes: OS-level access required; participant permissions and battery constraints may reduce sampling.

predictors.label: Time-awareness checks / time-estimation practice
predictors.query_text_used: Time-awareness checks / time-estimation practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Frequent clock-checks, countdowns or brief time-estimation practices recalibrate subjective time and reduce unnoticed long off-task periods. | Intervention examples: Set visible countdown timers, pause to estimate elapsed time every 10–20 minutes. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, how often did you check the time or use a countdown to monitor elapsed time? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Constantly) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_and_metacognitive_interventions / cognitive_remediation_and_rehabilitation / *time_estimation_strategy_training

mapping.rationale: Direct mapping: Time-awareness checks / time-estimation practice corresponds to the leaf time_estimation_strategy_training under PSYCHO / cognitive_and_metacognitive_interventions / cognitive_remediation_and_rehabilitation. The variable label aligns with time-estimation strategy training, and the EMA self-report measure item (frequency of time checks or countdown use since the last prompt) matches the leaf’s domain. Mechanism aligns: frequent time checks recalibrate subjective time and reduce unnoticed off-task periods. Pseudoprofile context supports selecting time-awareness as a modifiable cognitive strategy and prioritizes non-redundant predictors; this leaf is the most specific operational construct for the described variable.

source_variable.label: Time-awareness checks / time-estimation practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Frequent clock-checks, countdowns or brief time-estimation practices recalibrate subjective time and reduce unnoticed long off-task periods.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Set visible countdown timers, pause to estimate elapsed time every 10–20 minutes.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how often did you check the time or use a countdown to monitor elapsed time? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Constantly)
measurement.notes: Can be augmented with clock-app open counts if available.

expected_effects.rationale: More frequent time checks directly reduce loss of time awareness in the near term.
Better time-awareness supports timely initiation and return to task.
Reduces long immersion in substitutes by prompting re-evaluation of elapsed time.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA item; clock-app opens may be noisy across device types.

predictors.label: Contingency planning / deadline reframing (cognitive restructuring)
predictors.query_text_used: Contingency planning / deadline reframing (cognitive restructuring) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Pre-emptive planning and cognitive reframing reduce acute deadline-triggered panic by converting threat into manageable steps. | Intervention examples: Brief reappraisal of deadline meaning (e.g., 'I can prioritize the next immediate step') and if-then contingency plans. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, to what extent did you do any contingency planning or reframe upcoming deadlines (e.g., plan next steps, reappraise) ? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Very much) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / restructure_and_install / *cognitive_reframing_of_intentionality

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is 4174: cognitive_reframing_of_intentionality. The variable is Contingency planning / deadline reframing (cognitive restructuring) within PSYCHO domain. The ontology path shows restructure_and_install / *cognitive_reframing_of_intentionality, which directly corresponds to reframing deadline-related intent and planning steps (e.g., if-then plans, prioritizing next steps). Measurement (EMA self-report: since last prompt, extent of contingency planning or deadline reframing) aligns with cognitive reframing of intentionality as the mechanism to convert deadlines into manageable steps. Therefore this leaf provides the most specific operational construct for the variable.
mapping.leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / restructure_and_install / belief_strength_modulation / cognitive_reframing_of_intentionality

source_variable.label: Contingency planning / deadline reframing (cognitive restructuring)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Pre-emptive planning and cognitive reframing reduce acute deadline-triggered panic by converting threat into manageable steps.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief reappraisal of deadline meaning (e.g., 'I can prioritize the next immediate step') and if-then contingency plans.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you do any contingency planning or reframe upcoming deadlines (e.g., plan next steps, reappraise) ? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Very much)
measurement.notes: Targets the cognitive step expected to reduce panic when deadlines appear.

expected_effects.rationale: Reappraisal and contingency plans directly reduce acute deadline-related anxiety in the short term.
Contingency planning may increase perceived ability to act, thereby improving initiation (sign may be indirect; increased planning can precede action).
Reduces uncertainty/self-blame by creating concrete next steps.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Cognitive restructuring items could transiently raise distress if recalling stress; ensure supportive instructions.

feasibility.notes: Short EMA question acceptable; therapist-guided training may increase effect sizes later.

predictors.label: Self-compassion / uncertainty-processing practice
predictors.query_text_used: Self-compassion / uncertainty-processing practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Self-compassion reduces self-blame and rumination, supporting adaptive selection of corrective steps and reducing avoidance. | Intervention examples: Short self-compassion prompts (e.g., 'What would I say to a friend in this situation?'). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, to what extent did you practice self-compassion or intentionally reduce self-blame? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Very much) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / behavioral_integration / self_compassion_after_failure_practice

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is self_compassion_after_failure_practice (idx 2448). The variable label 'Self-compassion / uncertainty-processing practice' aligns with practicing self-compassion after challenges, and the EMA item 'Since the last prompt, to what extent did you practice self-compassion or intentionally reduce self-blame?' directly maps to this predictor. Mechanism: self-compassion reduces self-blame and rumination, supporting adaptive corrective actions after failure or uncertainty. The leaf is under behavioral_integration, fitting the pseudoprofile emphasis on modifiable self-management behaviors. This provides a specific, non-redundant self-compassion practice construct aligned with the measurement approach.

source_variable.label: Self-compassion / uncertainty-processing practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Self-compassion reduces self-blame and rumination, supporting adaptive selection of corrective steps and reducing avoidance.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short self-compassion prompts (e.g., 'What would I say to a friend in this situation?').

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you practice self-compassion or intentionally reduce self-blame? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Very much)
measurement.notes: Captures active practice (not trait self-compassion).

expected_effects.rationale: Directly targets self-blame and uncertainty, reducing their intensity.
Reduces emotional reactivity to deadlines via affect regulation.
By lowering avoidance, may indirectly improve initiation (effect may be indirect).

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: May briefly surface negative self-evaluations; framing should be supportive.

predictors.label: External cognitive offloading / checklists
predictors.query_text_used: External cognitive offloading / checklists | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Using checklists or stepwise external supports reduces reliance on internal working memory and lowers activation energy for task starts. | Intervention examples: Use a 3-step checklist posted near work space (e.g., open doc, write title, write first sentence). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, to what extent did you use an external checklist or offloading tool to start/structure the task? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Used and followed closely) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_capacity_enhancement_and_stimulation / cognitive_offloading_and_external_aids / *single_task_context_setup_strategy

mapping.rationale: Best match among candidates: PSYCHO / cognitive_capacity_enhancement_and_stimulation / cognitive_offloading_and_external_aids / external_memory_systems / design_and_use / checklists_for_error_prevention. This leaf explicitly encodes external memory aids via checklists, aligning with the variable label 'External cognitive offloading / checklists' and the measurement item that asks about use of an external checklist to start/structure a task. The measurement is EMA self-report, 1–9 Likert, and the mechanism aligns with reducing reliance on internal working memory and lowering activation energy for task initiation. It is the most specific operational construct among the candidates for this predictor.

source_variable.label: External cognitive offloading / checklists
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Using checklists or stepwise external supports reduces reliance on internal working memory and lowers activation energy for task starts.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Use a 3-step checklist posted near work space (e.g., open doc, write title, write first sentence).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you use an external checklist or offloading tool to start/structure the task? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Used and followed closely)
measurement.notes: Designed to capture external scaffolding use.

expected_effects.rationale: Offloading reduces friction to initiating tasks.
Structured steps channel behavior away from substitutes.
Tangible progress reduces self-blame via feedback.

predictors.label: Current energy / alertness level
predictors.query_text_used: Current energy / alertness level | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Momentary energy/alertness moderates ability to initiate and resist low-effort substitutes; influenced by sleep, nutrition and circadian factors. | Intervention examples: Short behavioral changes: hydration, brief movement break, or short nap (outside work contexts) to restore energy. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Right now (since the last prompt), how would you rate your energy/alertness? (1 = Exhausted, 9 = Very high energy) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Glymphatic_Support / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices (proximal energy measure)

mapping.rationale: Variable label is Current energy / alertness level. Measurement item matches exactly: 'Right now (since the last prompt), how would you rate your energy/alertness? (1 = Exhausted, 9 = Very high energy)'. The most specific operational predicate in the candidates is ema_mental_energy (ecological_momentary_assessment_ema / momentary_cognition_and_energy / ema_mental_energy), which aligns with momentary energy assessment via EMA in the BIO domain. This leaf is under ecological_momentary_assessment_ema, reflecting a proximal energy measure compatible with the pseudoprofile's emphasis on momentary energy as a moderator of initiation and distractibility. Thus it is the best-matching, most specific predictor node for this variable.

source_variable.label: Current energy / alertness level
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Momentary energy/alertness moderates ability to initiate and resist low-effort substitutes; influenced by sleep, nutrition and circadian factors.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short behavioral changes: hydration, brief movement break, or short nap (outside work contexts) to restore energy.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now (since the last prompt), how would you rate your energy/alertness? (1 = Exhausted, 9 = Very high energy)
measurement.notes: Energy is a proximal moderator for initiation and distractibility.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; consider controlling for circadian time-of-day

expected_effects.rationale: Higher energy is expected to reduce failure to initiate in the short term.
Low energy increases reliance on low-effort substitutes.
Better energy improves sustained attention against digital pulls.

feasibility.notes: Short momentary item with high compliance expected.

predictors.label: Last-night sleep quality and duration (morning diary)
predictors.query_text_used: Last-night sleep quality and duration (morning diary) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Sleep influences daytime cognitive control and energy; poor sleep can increase distractibility and reduce initiation. | Intervention examples: Consistent sleep schedule, sleep hygiene changes (no electronics before bed), environmental adjustments. | Measurement: other | Last night: how many hours did you sleep? ______ minutes. How would you rate your sleep quality? (1 = Very poor, 9 = Very good) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Glymphatic_Support / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Selected leaf 4549: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / self_assessment_and_logs / daily_and_retrospective_logs / sleep_diary. This directly encodes 'last-night sleep quality and duration' as a morning diary self-report. It provides both duration and quality via a diary-style self-assessment, aligning with the var's measurement item (hours slept, sleep quality 1-9) and the morning diary channel used in EMA. The variable label and mechanism (sleep influences daytime cognitive control and energy) are matched by this sleep_diary construct, which also fits the pseudoprofile's emphasis on high-signal, modifiable self-monitoring with EMA. Other candidates (e.g., sleep_timing_log, circadian_sleep_tracking) are less precisely aligned with the 'morning diary' aspect and/or lack the explicit diary/log framing for last-night sleep.

source_variable.label: Last-night sleep quality and duration (morning diary)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Sleep influences daytime cognitive control and energy; poor sleep can increase distractibility and reduce initiation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Consistent sleep schedule, sleep hygiene changes (no electronics before bed), environmental adjustments.

measurement.channel: morning diary in smartphone EMA app
measurement.item_or_signal: Last night: how many hours did you sleep? ______ minutes. How would you rate your sleep quality? (1 = Very poor, 9 = Very good)
measurement.notes: Use duration + quality; primary lag expected 0-24h to daytime outcomes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: duration: none or standardized; quality: z-score; control for weekday effects

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces failure to initiate across the next day.
Improves self-control against low-effort substitutions the following day.
Improves sustained attention.
Better sleep reduces time-loss episodes.

feasibility.notes: Single morning diary item; high expected completion if kept brief.
